title: England and the United States: two countries divided by a common law
gist: Legal writer DAT Green argues that this week's US Supreme Court ruling in Trump v Slaughter — upholding the president's power to fire an FTC commissioner despite a for-cause statute — shows US constitutional law has diverged sharply from its English common-law roots, where statute always trumps royal prerogative; the inconsistent same-day ruling in Trump v Cook (protecting a Fed governor) reveals the Court's conservative majority is engaged in a deliberate, ideological project to make the presidency dominant over Congress.

themes.theme: Trump v Slaughter: executive power upheld

insights.claim: In Trump v Slaughter, decided this week, the US Supreme Court's majority ruled that Congress's statutory requirement that a Federal Trade Commission commissioner can only be removed 'for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office' is unconstitutional because it fetters the president's executive power, upholding Trump's dismissal of FTC commissioner Rebecca Slaughter.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Establishes a precedent that independent-agency for-cause removal protections can be struck down, materially reducing regulatory-agency independence from the executive across the FTC and likely other independent bodies.
insights.quote: for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office
insights.locator: In Trump v Slaughter, the issue was whether to uphold a law of Congress

insights.claim: The president's legal position in the Slaughter case was that he could remove a commissioner at will, for any reason or no reason at all — not merely that the statutory for-cause conditions were satisfied — and the Supreme Court's majority sided with this maximalist view of unilateral removal power.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Marks a maximalist expansion of unitary-executive theory rather than a narrow ruling — relevant for forecasting future challenges to independent agencies' (SEC, NLRB) removal protections.
insights.quote: the president can remove a commissioner at will, for any reason or no reason at all
insights.locator: The president submitted that even these statutory conditions were objectionable

themes.theme: England's contrary rule: statute beats prerogative

insights.claim: Under English constitutional law the royal prerogative (through which UK executive power is exercised) can be limited by parliamentary statute, and where Parliament has legislated a for-cause removal requirement like the one in Trump v Slaughter, English courts would uphold the statute — primary legislation always prevails over royal/executive prerogative power when the two conflict.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Identifies the structural legal divergence: UK courts subordinate executive prerogative to statute, the opposite of the Slaughter outcome — a benchmark for comparing UK vs US regulatory/agency independence risk.
insights.quote: If primary legislation and royal power conflict, the latter always has to yield to the former.
insights.locator: In England, the corresponding constitutional and legal power rests with the royal prerogative

themes.theme: A deliberate constitutional project, not simple partisanship

insights.claim: Trump v Slaughter follows the Supreme Court's 2024 ruling granting the president the 'widest possible immunity' from criminal liability, forming part of a sequence of decisions expanding presidential power.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Places Slaughter in a multi-year pattern of executive-power-expanding rulings, useful for modeling the trajectory of further Court decisions on presidential authority.
insights.quote: widest possible immunity from criminal liability
insights.locator: The case of Trump v Slaughter, of course, follows the 2024 Supreme Court judgment

insights.claim: Even Supreme Court rulings that go against Trump, such as the recent tariff judgment, contain significant elements restricting the powers of Congress rather than the presidency — showing the Court's pattern is about shifting power from Congress to the executive branch generally, not simple pro-Trump partisanship.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Reframes the Court's jurisprudence as an institutional power shift toward the presidency (relevant across any future administration), not a Trump-specific bias — a durable structural risk for modeling US regulatory/agency stability.
insights.locator: And even Supreme Court cases which go against the president

insights.claim: DAT Green characterizes the Supreme Court's conservative-majority project as 'more profoundly ideological than personally partisan' — a deliberate, judgment-by-judgment refashioning of the US constitution to make the presidency more dominant than Congress.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: The author's central interpretive claim: treat the Court's agenda as a durable constitutional realignment rather than a temporary Trump-era anomaly when assessing long-run US regulatory and institutional risk.
insights.quote: more profoundly ideological than personally partisan. It is a political move of the deepest order.
insights.locator: Judgment by judgment, the Supreme Court's conservative majority

insights.claim: In Trump v Cook, decided the same day as Trump v Slaughter and involving an almost identical removal-power question but concerning a Federal Reserve governor, the Supreme Court found the president did NOT have absolute dismissal power — with reasoning the author says 'cannot be reconciled' with the Slaughter ruling.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A direct doctrinal inconsistency carved out specifically to protect the Federal Reserve's independence — signals the Court is treating Fed independence as a special case, relevant for monetary-policy-independence risk even as other agencies lose removal protections.
insights.locator: In Trump v Cook the issue was almost identical

insights.claim: The Supreme Court gave no clear legal principle for why the Federal Reserve was treated as 'special' and exempted from the executive-power logic applied to the FTC in Slaughter, a gap the author predicts will be an unresolvable exam question for generations of law students.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: The unexplained Fed carve-out is a legal fault line — future litigants may test whether Fed independence protections actually hold up, given the Court supplied no principled distinction.
insights.quote: the court could not explain why as a matter of legal principle
insights.locator: For some reason the Federal Reserve bank was special

insights.claim: The author is skeptical that the US Supreme Court has 'a fair and accurate understanding' of the English common-law basis of the US constitution, comparing its selective citation of Blackstone's commentaries to people picking biblical verses to justify preconceived conclusions.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Casts doubt on the legitimacy of the Court's historical-common-law reasoning technique itself, not just its conclusions — relevant to broader skepticism about originalist/historical-analogy jurisprudence.
insights.quote: paragraphs of Blackstone's commentaries are selected in the same way that some people select biblical verses to justify preconceived ideas
insights.locator: Some would doubt that the US Supreme Court

insights.claim: The author concludes that a court quashing legislation because it inconveniences the executive is 'not what many would expect' as the United States marks 250 years of independence from royal control — an ironic reversal given the country's founding rejection of unchecked executive/monarchical power.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Frames the long-run irony/thesis of the piece — a shorthand for the structural direction of US executive power just as the 250th-anniversary narrative is being publicly celebrated.
insights.quote: the quashing of legislation because it inconveniences the executive is not what many would expect
insights.locator: But taking this common heritage at its highest
